caption: Fig. 1: Uncertainty-guided XAI resource allocation via epistemic gating. Epis- temic uncertainty is obtained from the model’s native estimator or a lightweight surrogate. Under constrained computation budget, Use Case 1 routes samples to low- or high-cost XAI methods based on expected reliability; Use Case 2 defers high-uncertainty samples, retaining only reliable explanations.
description2: Uncertainty-guided XAI resource allocation via epistemic gating is illustrated, using model-native estimators or surrogates for episodic uncertainty.
description3: The multi-panel plot, structured as a flowchart, outlines uncertainty-guided XAI resource allocation with two use cases.
questions: How does the approach manage resource constraints when generating explanations?
What are the different strategies for handling samples with varying reliability?

caption: Fig. 2: Correlation heatmaps of XAI stability and epistemic uncertainty (XEC) under natural (left) and adversarial (right) perturbations. Each cell reports Spearman correlation between epistemic growth (EG) and explanation degra- dation (XD) across perturbation strengths for a given dataset, model and XAI method. Blue and gray regions (XEC < −0.6) correspond to strong negative association, where UQ serves as a reliable proxy for XAI instability.
description2: Correlation heatmaps of XAI stability and epistemic uncertainty under natural and adversarial perturbations are presented.
description3: Two-panel heatmaps display Spearman correlations between epistemic growth and explanation degradation across datasets, models, and perturbation strengths, colored blue to gray.
questions: How do adversarial perturbations influence the association between explanation and uncertainty?
What general trends are visible regarding explanation degradation and instability?

caption: Fig. 3: Stratified SHAP stability (Kendall’s τ) across epistemic uncertainty bins under Gaussian noise (σ) for low (blue), medium (green), and high (red) epis- temic strata. Violin plots show the distribution of stability values. Dots indicate means, horizontal bars the median, and vertical bars the standard deviation.
description2: Stratified SHAP stability is plotted across epistemic uncertainty bins under Gaussian noise for low, medium, and high strata, with violin plots showing value distributions.
description3: Four-panel plot displays violin plots showing stability values (Kendall’s τ) for low, medium, and high epistemic strata across three categories.
questions: What do the bars and violin plots indicate about data distribution?
How do stability values differ between the epistemic strata?

caption: Fig. 4: Prediction shift (MSE in log-odds space) after removing top-k SHAP features by low- (blue), high- (red), and random-epistemic (gray) groups. Low- epistemic samples show larger shifts, indicating more faithful explanations.
description2: The figure plots the prediction shift in terms of mean squared error in log-odds space after removing top-k SHAP features by low-, high-, and random-epistemic groups.
description3: Two-panel plot displays mean squared error (MSE) changes after removing top-k SHAP features, using blue, red, and gray bars for low, high, and random groups.
questions: How does removing features affect prediction accuracy for different epistemic groups?
What is the general trend in prediction shift as more features are removed?

caption: Fig. 5: Epistemic uncertainty (left axis, blue) and SSIM stability (right axis, or- ange) means and standard deviations versus noise level (bottom axis). Axes are color-coded. As noise increases, uncertainty rises while SSIM decreases mono- tonically (XEC ρ = −1.0 for both IG and SG).
description2: The figure plots the mean and standard deviation of epistemic uncertainty (blue) and SSIM stability (orange) against noise level, with color-coded axes.
description3: The line chart displays epistemic uncertainty and SSIM stability means and standard deviations versus noise level, color-coded by group.
questions: How do epistemic uncertainty and SSIM stability change with increasing noise levels?
What is the general relationship between the two metrics shown as noise increases?

caption: Fig. 6: PlantVillage: IG and SG saliency maps for clean and noisy low-epistemic (rows 1, 3) and high-epistemic (rows 2, 4) samples. For visualization, heatmaps are lightly Gaussian-smoothed to reduce pixel noise and emphasize structural patterns. Low-epistemic explanations maintain focused attribution on leaf struc- ture and disease areas.
description2: The figure displays IG and SG saliency maps for plant samples with varying epistemic levels, featuring heatmaps smoothed to highlight structural patterns.
description3: The figure presents a multi-panel plot of heatmaps, showing saliency maps for clean and noisy samples across different epistemic levels.
questions: How do explanations differ for low-epistemic versus high-epistemic samples?
What structural patterns are emphasized by the Gaussian-smoothed heatmaps?

caption: Fig. 7: Epistemic uncertainty vs. SHAP stability (Kendall’s τ) under pooled noise conditions. Green points denote stable explanations (τ ≥ 0.7), red points de- note unstable. Vertical dashed lines show 30%, 50%, and 70% deferral rates ν. Marginal distributions illustrate class separation along each axis.
description2: The figure plots epistemic uncertainty against SHAP stability, with green points representing stable explanations and red points denoting unstable ones under pooled noise conditions.
description3: A scatter plot displays epistemic uncertainty versus SHAP stability (Kendall’s τ) for wine (left) and bean (right) data, with marginal distributions shown.
questions: How do conditions affect the stability of explanations?
What is the relationship between epistemic uncertainty and explanation stability?

caption: Fig. 8: Attribution mass on signal (blue) vs. noise (red) features across epistemic groups (solid: low, hatched: random, cross-hatched: high) and noise feature ra- tios. Low-epistemic explanations maintain focus on signal features better.
description2: The figure plots the attribution mass of signal versus noise features across epistemic groups with varying noise ratios, shown in solid, hatched, and cross-hatched patterns.
description3: Three-panel plots display signal (blue) and noise (red) feature attribution across epistemic groups and noise ratios for random forest and logistic regression.
questions: How do different epistemic groups attribute signal versus noise features?
What general patterns are visible in the distribution of attribution mass?
